data.title: Dollarama (TSX:DOL) Stock Faces Margin Decline That Tests Bullish Growth Narrative
data.description:
Dollarama (TSX:DOL) opened its 2027 financial year with Q1 revenue of C$1.8 billion and basic EPS of C$1.11, while trailing twelve month EPS sat at C$4.88 on revenue of C$7.6 billion. Over the past year, revenue on a trailing basis moved from C$6.5 billion to C$7.6 billion and EPS increased from C$4.39 to C$4.88, setting the backdrop for investors watching how this latest quarter fits into the broader trend. With net margin over the last twelve months at 17.6%, down from 18.8% a year earlier, the focus is now on how Dollarama balances top line and EPS progress with pressure on profitability.

See our full analysis for Dollarama.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how these results line up against the most common narratives around Dollarama’s growth, risks, and profitability.

See what the community is saying about Dollarama

Advertisement

Same store sales up 5.6% in Q1

  • Q1 2027 same store sales grew 5.6%, compared with 4.9% in Q1 2026 and 6.0% in Q3 2026, while total Q1 revenue reached C$1.8b versus C$1.5b a year earlier.
  • Supporters of the bullish view point to this kind of mid single digit same store sales growth as evidence of a resilient value proposition, yet the reported margin trend introduces some friction with that story.
    • Bulls highlight recurring traffic and transaction frequency, and the 12 month revenue base of C$7.6b versus C$6.5b a year earlier lines up with the idea that Dollarama is capturing more spend over time.
    • At the same time, the 12 month net margin moved from 18.8% to 17.6%, so while top line growth aligns with the bullish narrative, profitability is not moving in the same direction that more optimistic margin assumptions would imply.

Bulls argue that consistent same store sales growth could justify paying up for Dollarama if margins recover, so it is worth checking how the full bullish case stacks up against the numbers just reported 🐂 Dollarama Bull Case

Net margin slips to 17.6%

  • On a trailing 12 month basis, Dollarama generated C$1.3b of net income on C$7.6b of revenue, for a 17.6% net margin that is below the 18.8% margin reported a year earlier.
  • Critics in the bearish camp focus on rising wage and compliance costs and question how durable margins are, and the recent numbers give some support to that caution without showing a sharp break in profitability.
    • Bears point to higher labor costs in Australia and Latin America and the risk that operating expenses absorb more of each sales dollar, and the 1.2 percentage point margin move over the last 12 months is consistent with some pressure on earnings quality.
    • However, trailing EPS of C$4.88 is still above the C$4.39 level a year earlier, so even with margin compression, absolute earnings continue to grow and that tempers the more pessimistic view that profitability could be structurally impaired.

Skeptics warn that if cost pressures persist, recent margin compression could matter more than today’s earnings growth, so it can be useful to see how the full cautious narrative frames these risks against the current results 🐻 Dollarama Bear Case

Premium P/E and DCF gap stand out

  • Dollarama trades on a P/E of 38.6x, well above the peer average of 22.8x and the Global Multiline Retail industry at 18.6x, while the current share price of C$190.94 also sits above a DCF fair value of C$138.92.
  • Consensus narrative flags this combination of strong growth and a rich multiple as the core trade off, and the latest figures show why both sides of that equation are in focus.
    • On the growth side, earnings increased 9.1% over the last year and the five year earnings compound rate of 17.3% per year supports the idea that the business has grown meaningfully faster than many broad market references.
    • On the valuation side, comparing the C$190.94 share price with a DCF fair value of C$138.92 and an analyst price target of C$209.18 shows the market is already assigning a premium relative to both a cash flow model and the industry multiple, so any change in growth or margins can have an outsized effect on how comfortable that premium feels.

data.title: Wells Fargo (WFC) Stock After 3-Year Surge Is There Still Value Here
data.description:
• Investors may be wondering whether Wells Fargo at around US$83.73 is offering good value right now, or if the easy money has already been made.
  • The stock has returned 2.2% over the past week and 13.9% over the past month, while year to date it is down 12.0%. It sits on a 1 year return of 18.3%, a 3 year return of 113.9% and a 5 year return of 126.2%.
  • Recent headlines have focused on Wells Fargo's ongoing efforts to strengthen its franchise and address past regulatory and operational issues, alongside commentary on its position among large U.S. banks. This backdrop helps frame how investors are currently thinking about both its risks and its potential.
  • Simply Wall St's valuation model currently gives Wells Fargo a score of 4/6. This score will be unpacked using several valuation approaches, and later the article will look at a more holistic way to judge whether that score really tells the full story.

Find out why Wells Fargo's 18.3% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Wells Fargo Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much value Wells Fargo can create over and above the return that equity investors require, and then capitalizes those surplus returns into an intrinsic value per share.

For Wells Fargo, the starting point is its Book Value of $53.21 per share and an Average Return on Equity of 13.35%. Based on analyst inputs, the model uses a Stable EPS of $7.98 per share, sourced from weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 17 analysts. The required return for shareholders, or Cost of Equity, is $4.79 per share, so the Excess Return is $3.19 per share.

These excess returns are projected on a Stable Book Value of $59.79 per share, which comes from weighted future Book Value estimates from 14 analysts, and then discounted back to today. This produces an intrinsic value estimate of about $131.14 per share.

Compared with the current share price of around $83.73, the model implies an intrinsic discount of 36.2%, which points to Wells Fargo stock trading at a substantial discount to this Excess Returns estimate.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Wells Fargo Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Wells Fargo, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand for what investors are currently willing to pay for each dollar of earnings. It helps you compare the stock with other banks and with its own earnings power.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually points to a lower multiple.

Wells Fargo currently trades on a P/E of 12.40x, compared with an industry average for banks of about 11.84x and a peer average of 13.91x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Wells Fargo is 14.18x. This Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of what the P/E might be, given factors such as the company’s earnings profile, its industry, profit margins, market capitalization and specific risks.

Because the Fair Ratio incorporates these company specific drivers, it offers a more tailored reference point than simple peer or industry comparisons. With the Fair Ratio above the current P/E, this approach points to Wells Fargo trading below that Fair Ratio benchmark.

data.title: Chipotle (CMG) Stock After 36% Twelve-Month Slide Is The Pullback Enough Yet
data.description:

Chipotle Mexican Grill scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Approach 1: Chipotle Mexican Grill Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a stock could be worth today by projecting future cash flows and discounting them back to the present using a required return. It is essentially asking what those future dollars are worth in today’s terms.

For Chipotle Mexican Grill, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections in $. The company’s last twelve months Free Cash Flow is about $1.53b. Analyst estimates and Simply Wall St extrapolations project annual Free Cash Flow into the next decade, with discounted forecasts such as $1.38b in 2026 and $1.47b in 2028, rising to discounted figures of $1.15b in 2035.

When all those discounted cash flows are added up, the model produces an estimated intrinsic value of $28.34 per share. Compared with the current share price, the DCF output indicates the stock is about 13.7% above this estimate, which points to Chipotle Mexican Grill trading at a premium to this cash flow based valuation.

Result: OVERVALUED

For a profitable company, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to link what you pay for the stock with the earnings it produces. It gives you a quick sense of how many years of current earnings the market is pricing in.

What counts as a "normal" P/E depends on how fast earnings are expected to grow and how risky those earnings are. Higher expected growth and lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually points to a lower P/E being reasonable.

Chipotle Mexican Grill currently trades on a P/E of 28.47x. That is above the Hospitality industry average of about 22.57x, yet below the peer group average of 35.30x. data.title: We're Keeping An Eye On Aspire Mining's (ASX:AKM) Cash Burn Rate
data.description:
There's no doubt that money can be made by owning shares of unprofitable businesses. For example, biotech and mining exploration companies often lose money for years before finding success with a new treatment or mineral discovery. Having said that, unprofitable companies are risky because they could potentially burn through all their cash and become distressed.

Given this risk, we thought we'd take a look at whether Aspire Mining (ASX:AKM) shareholders should be worried about its cash burn. For the purposes of this article, cash burn is the annual rate at which an unprofitable company spends cash to fund its growth; its negative free cash flow. First, we'll determine its cash runway by comparing its cash burn with its cash reserves.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Advertisement

How Long Is Aspire Mining's Cash Runway?

A company's cash runway is calculated by dividing its cash hoard by its cash burn. As at December 2025, Aspire Mining had cash of US$5.0m and no debt. Looking at the last year, the company burnt through US$8.6m. So it had a cash runway of approximately 7 months from December 2025. That's quite a short cash runway, indicating the company must either reduce its annual cash burn or replenish its cash. You can see how its cash balance has changed over time in the image below.

See our latest analysis for Aspire Mining

How Is Aspire Mining's Cash Burn Changing Over Time?

Because Aspire Mining isn't currently generating revenue, we consider it an early-stage business. So while we can't look to sales to understand growth, we can look at how the cash burn is changing to understand how expenditure is trending over time. The skyrocketing cash burn up 160% year on year certainly tests our nerves. It's fair to say that sort of rate of increase cannot be maintained for very long, without putting pressure on the balance sheet. Admittedly, we're a bit cautious of Aspire Mining due to its lack of significant operating revenues. We prefer most of the stocks on this list of stocks that analysts expect to grow.

How Easily Can Aspire Mining Raise Cash?

Given its cash burn trajectory, Aspire Mining shareholders should already be thinking about how easy it might be for it to raise further cash in the future. Generally speaking, a listed business can raise new cash through issuing shares or taking on debt. Many companies end up issuing new shares to fund future growth. We can compare a company's cash burn to its market capitalisation to get a sense for how many new shares a company would have to issue to fund one year's operations.

Aspire Mining has a market capitalisation of US$89m and burnt through US$8.6m last year, which is 9.7% of the company's market value. Given that is a rather small percentage, it would probably be really easy for the company to fund another year's growth by issuing some new shares to investors, or even by taking out a loan.

So, Should We Worry About Aspire Mining's Cash Burn?

On this analysis of Aspire Mining's cash burn, we think its cash burn relative to its market cap was reassuring, while its increasing cash burn has us a bit worried. Considering all the measures mentioned in this report, we reckon that its cash burn is fairly risky, and if we held shares we'd be watching like a hawk for any deterioration. Separately, we looked at different risks affecting the company and spotted 5 warning signs for Aspire Mining (of which 3 make us uncomfortable!) you should know about.

data.title: We Think Softfront Holdings (TSE:2321) Can Easily Afford To Drive Business Growth
data.description:
We can readily understand why investors are attracted to unprofitable companies. For example, although Amazon.com made losses for many years after listing, if you had bought and held the shares since 1999, you would have made a fortune. Having said that, unprofitable companies are risky because they could potentially burn through all their cash and become distressed.

Given this risk, we thought we'd take a look at whether Softfront Holdings (TSE:2321) shareholders should be worried about its cash burn. For the purpose of this article, we'll define cash burn as the amount of cash the company is spending each year to fund its growth (also called its negative free cash flow). The first step is to compare its cash burn with its cash reserves, to give us its 'cash runway'.

Advertisement

When Might Softfront Holdings Run Out Of Money?

A company's cash runway is calculated by dividing its cash hoard by its cash burn. As at March 2026, Softfront Holdings had cash of JP¥1.7b and no debt. Looking at the last year, the company burnt through JP¥283m. That means it had a cash runway of about 6.0 years as of March 2026. Even though this is but one measure of the company's cash burn, the thought of such a long cash runway warms our bellies in a comforting way. Depicted below, you can see how its cash holdings have changed over time.

View our latest analysis for Softfront Holdings

Is Softfront Holdings' Revenue Growing?

Given that Softfront Holdings actually had positive free cash flow last year, before burning cash this year, we'll focus on its operating revenue to get a measure of the business trajectory. Although it's hardly brilliant growth, it's good to see the company grew revenue by 17% in the last year. Of course, we've only taken a quick look at the stock's growth metrics, here. This graph of historic earnings and revenue shows how Softfront Holdings is building its business over time.

How Hard Would It Be For Softfront Holdings To Raise More Cash For Growth?

While Softfront Holdings is showing solid revenue growth, it's still worth considering how easily it could raise more cash, even just to fuel faster growth. Generally speaking, a listed business can raise new cash through issuing shares or taking on debt. Many companies end up issuing new shares to fund future growth. By comparing a company's annual cash burn to its total market capitalisation, we can estimate roughly how many shares it would have to issue in order to run the company for another year (at the same burn rate).

Since it has a market capitalisation of JP¥6.7b, Softfront Holdings' JP¥283m in cash burn equates to about 4.2% of its market value. Given that is a rather small percentage, it would probably be really easy for the company to fund another year's growth by issuing some new shares to investors, or even by taking out a loan.

Is Softfront Holdings' Cash Burn A Worry?

It may already be apparent to you that we're relatively comfortable with the way Softfront Holdings is burning through its cash. In particular, we think its cash runway stands out as evidence that the company is well on top of its spending. And even though its revenue growth wasn't quite as impressive, it was still a positive.
